metric_value: Thin-client Web browser (e.g., Netscape); WAN/LAN using TCP/IP; HTTP server; CGI scripts; firewall for enterprise-network privacy; application RDBMS.
Client-server: app runs on desktop; data brought from server to desktop; session-oriented (OLTP). Internet: app runs on server(s); presentation by client browser; no concept of a session.
Thin-client Web browser as presentation layer enables application architecture that runs on servers and presents via browser — foundational framing for the next decade of enterprise apps.
CGI positioned as current server-side scripting standard; implicitly foreshadows need for richer application servers.
Web application architecture (server-side app + browser presentation) became the dominant enterprise-app architecture by ~2001 and remains so. Kastner prediction verified.
CGI was displaced by application servers (Java EE, PHP, Rails, Node, etc.) within a decade; Kastner's implicit pointer to limitations of raw CGI was correct.
Client-server architecture was largely displaced by Web/web-services architecture across enterprise app development by 2005. Kastner's 1996 contrast framing was prescient.
Netscape referenced as the default example browser in a June-1996 CA sales deck — accurate market position at time of writing.

notes: Title progression: VP (1996) -> Group VP (1997) -> EVP (1998) -> Founder & CRO (2001).

metric_name: Kastner delivering sales training for CA
metric_value: June 1996 Internet app-dev training for CA sales — concrete evidence of the sales-force-education service line named in the 1999 retainer proposal.
